comments.title: Parapsychological research and magic
comments.content:
Have you looked at parapsychology researcher Dean Radin's latest book "Real Magic"? Among other things (including the usual sparring with Skeptics) "Real Magic" contains some discussion of experimental results that could in theory be of interest to practitioners of magic. I'm curious whether you think formal experimental science can be useful to magicians.

For example, Radin's book discusses some random number generator (RNG) experiments that asked participants what techniques they used as they tried to influence the RNGs with their minds. The most successful techniques for RNG-influencing were, from most effective to least effective:

"resonance [i.e., feeling 'at one' with the RNG], ... asking entities (spirits or angels) for assistance, using emotion to help 'power' the will, one-pointed concentration, physical relaxation, visual imagery, and finally, talking to the RNG as though it was a sentient creature..."

Are results like these of interest to magicians? Are they boringly obvious? Are the research methods fundamentally flawed? Is experimental science inimical to magic?

replies.title: Re: Parapsychological research and magic
replies.content:
I haven't read it; I don't follow parapsychology much. I appreciate what the parapsychologists are doing -- trying to show that magic, and things like it, really do work in the real world -- but those of us who practice magic by and large aren't interested in that; we know that it works, and our main interest is in getting good at it.

Imagine, by way of comparison, that there was a well-organized, well-funded lobby that insisted that it's impossible to cook tasty, edible meals at home -- you've got to go to a restaurant instead. (This is the traditional attitude of religious professionals toward magic, for what it's worth.) Of course it would be useful for people to set out to prove that yes, in fact, it's possible to cook dinner at home -- but those who'd already figured that out would be more interested in finding old cookbooks and learning to use the recipes than they would be in continuing to prove something they already knew to be true.

replies.title: Re: Parapsychological research and magic
replies.content:
Yes, the scientific method has one problem when it tries to validate magic.

Magic requires mind focus to work, and changing the focus will disrupt it. I can think of two examples:

Faery lore is full of this. Persons in several kinds of relationships with the Good People try to change them, like stealing something, or trying to prove they exist, etc etc... and this makes the fae go away, never to return.

You start to notice that your divination is returning statistically anomalous results, and then you change your focus to prove this. Jinx promptly ensues, and it stops working. You fill find examples of this if you dig into Greer's forums.

You might be interested in the book The Origins of Psychic Phenomena by Stan Gooch, originally published as Creatures from Inner Space. The final chapters discuss problems in parapsychology from a sympathetic perspective.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I am working through the druid magic handbook but am frequently in circumstances where it would be socially inappropriate to do the SoP out loud. On those days, I practice silently or at a whisper, while imagining myself speaking and vibrating out loud.

Is this the best way to adapt to the need to be inaudible, or are there other useful tricks?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's one way to do it. Another, which requires a little more practice, is called the Great Voice. To do this, when you would normally vibrate a word, imagine the vibration as intensely as possible, just as though you were actually making it; concentrate on feeling the familiar tingling or buzzing sensation you get when you vibrate a word. Once you learn the trick, you can get nearly the same effect in perfect silence as you'd get vibrating out loud.

Another approach is to do the working on the inner planes. Sit in a chair on the north side of the space, facing south, and imagine yourself standing up, going to the center of the space, and beginning the SoP. Imagine it all as clearly as possible -- the movements, the sounds, everything, as though you were doing the SoP physically, while your physical body remains silently in the chair. When you're finished, return to the chair. This is great training for pathworking and astral projection, too.

replies.title: Inner planes working
replies.content: I've done this--one snowy morning last winter I had to rush out of the house without time for the SoP, only to have the bus not show up. So, I decided to try performing the SoP in my Inner Grove (the one I'd envisioned for my Candidate initiation) while standing still at the bus stop. It was shockingly effective, and ever since then I've done all of my rituals on both the physical and inner planes--Sister Crow

comments.title: An ex-apprentice
comments.content:
I was apprentice/partner/room mate with a mentally unstable self-professed philosopher, alchemist, inventor, etc... Your assessment about learning one system well makes sense in light of his constant skimming and the results of his work. He sought to create his own system before mastering one. His practice seemed to be a hodgepodge of stuff from everywhere. He spent a large amount of his teens sleeping for extreme amounts of time and had a cosmology built out of that. He said most of his ideas came from his dreams though I often come across what seem to be influences.

He claimed he wanted to save to world and proceeded to do it by using everyone around him and then claiming they needed to adapt to him when they called him on his behavior. When you would ask him a question about something you didn't understand, he would start his spiel over verbatim instead of addressing the question. He hated being interrupted but did it all the time and would monologue you into submission. He would talk use rules of conflict resolution that only served him.

He had a lot of opportunities - loans, contacts, allies - but every group he formed seemed to implode or attract people actively trying to change its purpose which reminding me of your comments on Crowley. I found it difficult to detach because I wanted to be part of his work and associated with any spillover glory. Journey to the East by Herman Hesse makes sense here for me. I wanted to help publish his work as I was intrigued by the snippets I had read online that led us into relationship. He got me on a side project of his, I moved into his communal house to work on it, that was supposed to make us rich and lead the way to all other glories. I only did it because I wanted to publish the work and know everything that he knew. I was love-bombed / publicly shamed enough times that I was totally loyal.

Providence intervened in the way of a house fire that gave me the chance to move out of his house and create some distance. Down the line, I scanned his notes with his permission, he was worried about his life's work disappearing and wanted some redundancy and that created a new distance - I could read the work without having to talk with him. Yet now I'm not so sure because this is one the last links between us.

I read enough of his work to realize I didn't want any part of his mind. It was too disorganized. Too many diatribes, and unmarked figures. Too many asides and as the dates got more recent, a monomaniacal repetition of the project we were working on that had already burned half a dozen partners. And reading them made me think like him, but it was a lot easier to disassociate than to speak with him when he used "THE VOICE".

I said all this because you answered another post about Landmark that they may curse people who leave and he was an advocate of that organization. He sent it through the grapevine that he disapproved of my new profession, which I switched to largely out of frustration working with him and enjoy far more. His comments made me feel it was a good choice. I'm wondering if he used the mutual friend as an instrument. I'm wondering if he cursed me. I am still terrified to run into him or for him to meet my family and I can't explain why because he is not physically intimidating, but he scares me.

I've kept these files until recently, though I had no intention of ever reading them. Looking at them just made me angry. It took a long time to stop thinking through his eyes and to regain my confidence in my self. It took a long time to separate out what I liked what he said, and what I didn't, to fact check things I would just parrot without questioning. I didn't think I could ever be to taken like that and I learned the hard way. I'd like to believe it will never happen again. Only my wife, a couple of good friends and providence saved me.

Is there something I can do to rid myself of him for good beyond what I've done (deleting his work, ending the relationship, breaking contact)?
Will all the advice you gave regarding Landmark apply equally well to my circumstances?
Do you have any other comments about learning from someone inadequate to teach?

replies.title: Re: An ex-apprentice
replies.content:
I'm sorry you had to go through such an experience! Yes. the advice I gave about the Landmark curse would also be useful to you. If you can find (and can afford) counseling or therapy, that might also be a good idea -- it sounds as though you've been left with some significant psychological wounds.

More generally, your story may be helpful to other people who may have to deal with this sort of person. There are a lot of them out there -- people who use occultism or spirituality as a way to avoid dealing with personal problems and/or a way to manipulate other people. One useful piece of advice I got from a teacher many years back, and have found to be reliably true, is that you don't want to study magic from someone whose life is a smoking crater. Operative mages have bad days like everyone else, sure; they're rarely rich or influential, since the time and effort that's needed to become rich and influential gets devoted to studying and practicing magic instead -- but if someone who claims to be able to teach magic spends his or her life careening from one disaster to another, leaving a trail of wrecked relationships and failed projects behind, don't walk away, run.

replies.title: Re: An ex-apprentice
replies.content:
Thanks for the kind words. I'll be sure to try it.

Its been about five years since we last had contact and last night, as I was writing this post, I deleted the scans of his papers. I've been keeping them expecting I'd find something worthwhile in them some day, but I have to let go.

In terms of counseling, I can't afford it. I've done the best I can with friends, family, clergy, a healthy dose of the I Ching, your blog and lots of reflection.

One of my big take-away's was that you can't save the world, it doesn't need you to save it and even if it did, you couldn't. He was an advocate of releasing GMO's into the biosphere to boost the productivity of life and thought that if he didn't, we would be doomed. Basically in his worldview, the planets fate - good or bad - hinged on his actions. He could save it or inadvertently destroy it - but if he didn't the bad people would destroy it first.

For those people out there dealing with something similar - if your wife is suspicious that every other thing you say is from one source - he is not your friend.

replies.title: Re: An ex-apprentice
replies.content:
I understand about counseling; a friendly clergyperson is as good as anything else if he or she has the time to spare. It's mostly the chance to unburden yourself from the emotional impact of a bad relationship. You might also try journaling -- a lot of people find that writing out their feelings and their experiences in a journal or blank book can be just as healing. It means a lot to get it all down on paper.

The I Ching is another first-rate source of guidance! I don't know what your experience has been, but to me it's always had a decided personality, a wise and slightly amused elder who always has something thoughtful to say.

replies.title: Re: An ex-apprentice
replies.content:
(different anonymous poster here)

To the original poster, I'm so sorry to hear about this terrible experience, and I'm glad you've had the chance to escape!

While the details are different, I've been dealing with the fallout of my own version of this, getting away from an initially charming, magically talented, but ultimately manipulative and destructive partner.

While JMG specifically mentions that "you don't want to study magic from someone whose life is a smoking crater", I would suspect that the same note of caution applies to any intimate relationship (be it with a romantic partner, teacher, initiator, or what have you) with someone whose life is a smoking crater.

As I run (not walk) away, JMG, I'll certainly go back to find the Landmark thread and read your advice there. But are there any special "cutting" actions that you'd recommend, given that the relationship was sexual, and the special energetic bonds that go with that?

That's my big question. But relatedly, how would one best go about getting rid of the physical items left behind (letters, papers, gifts received, etc.) after the end of a situation like either of these? Burning, burying, dropping in a river, throwing in the trash, regifting where possible... all these come to mind, but would some be better than others for breaking the connections which the items represent and which they anchor in the material world? Fire, for example, seem like it has the potential to energize just as much as to destroy...

replies.title: Re: An ex-apprentice
replies.content:
When sex is involved, you've got some very close energetic bonds that need to be dissolved. Bathing in salt water -- cold salt water if you can stand that -- is a good start, but some aspects won't be resolved until you have another sexual relationship -- not a casual one, but one that stirs your emotions as well as your libido. Once you polarize with someone else, the old connection gives way.

With regard to getting rid of physical objects, giving something to charity is a good way to break the link -- it's a very old tradition that you can break the curse on an item by giving it to a beggar, and passing something over to a charity shop, which will use the proceeds to feed the poor or what have you, in my experience has the same effect. What can't be given to charity should be burnt if it can be burnt, and cast into running water if it can't be burnt. In either case, do it with intent: for example, you might say nine times, "(person's full name), I release you from my life. Go with my blessing." (The blessing's important because it helps take care of any lingering karma that may connect you.)

As for your note of caution, yes, absolutely. I've known a lot of people who got into relationships of one kind or another hoping to rescue the other person from the mess they'd made of their life. I have never -- as in not once -- seen any such relationship go well or end well.

comments.title: Coffee in Natural Magic

I know of two general ways to figure out how a plant might be useful in natural magic. 1. Some observed quality of the plant that can be associated with the goal of the spell, e.g., appearance of some part of the plant (Doctrine of Similars), its scent, something about its growing habits or its practical uses, or just hang out with the live plant and notice how it makes you feel. comments.title: Proper way to erect standing stones
comments.content: What would be the proper way to erect standing stones? I am staying in a town this week that erected a stone circle to reflect the Celtic heritage of early families and to act as a bit of a tourist trap. To put it bluntly, they f***ed it up. The stones 'scream' at me when I get close. This has been noted by a number of other people I've asked about it. My guess is that they were hauled out of somewhere they were very happy to be, quite possibly with significance to indigenous traditions.

replies.title: Re: Proper way to erect standing stones
replies.content:
We no longer know the proper way to erect standing stones. When megaliths were a living technology, there was an immense body of oral tradition governing their placement and use, and the creation and function of the energy paths connecting them. All that was lost thousands of years ago. The ancient Druids probably preserved some scraps of the lore they'd inherited from the pre-Celtic peoples who erected the stones, but next to nothing survives of their teachings; what we've got in modern Druidry has been painstakingly reinvented over the last three hundred years, and there's still a vast amount to be relearned.

It's possible to work around this by using divination to decide on the placement of stones; if I were called in as a Druid consultant to help guide the placement of a stone circle, that's certainly what I'd do! Lacking that, though, or someone with a really strong intuitive connection with the land energies, putting up standing stones in this day and age is a crapshoot; you're as likely to focus negative energies as positive ones. I've been to several modern stone circles that were placed without any such precautions being taken, and most of them have energies that range from the harsh to the creepy.

replies.title: Re: Proper way to erect standing stones
replies.content:
One tiny, tiny detail of the original praxis for erecting a standing stone **just might** have been rediscovered in modern times by accident.

Suppose that you have already prepared the hole (or "socket") for a tall standing stone, and the stone itself is lying horizontally on the earth with its base next to the socket. Then you can set the stone into the socket by slowly raising the end that is farthest away from the socket.

(You can do this by levering the end up by small increments and wedging small stones under the tall stone to hold it up off the ground. Thor Heyerdahl describes this in his account (in _Aku-Aku_) of how the natives on Easter Island actually re-erected one of the great stone statues to show him how it had been done in the past.)

Here's the thing that caught some folk by surprise when they actually saw it happen for the first time: at a certain point in the work, the weight of the stone itself helps move the process of setting it along. At that point in the work the stone actually rises up of its own weight, and seemingly of its own volition, and then it appears to *leap* into the socket like a living creature and settle itself there.

This is just a natural phenomenon having to do with the position of the center of gravity of the stone relative to the socket prepared for it. But it does look like the deliberate action of a living creature.

It is probably safe to assume that the ancient people who first erected their great standing stones saw the same thing happen, and drew from it the conclusion that the stone itself had chosen to take an active role in its own positioning. No doubt this then gave rise to rituals that treated the stone as a living creature.

Hints of the same thing may be preserved in the language of some Native American peoples, where stones are classified *grammatically* as living beings, together with plants and animals, and not as inanimate (or dead) objects like fish-hooks or sea-shells.

replies.title: Re: Proper way to erect standing stones
replies.content:
Most interesting! I recall reading something about this in an essay on the subject in Druid circles, but didn't know of the Thor Heyerdahl link.

The great challenge, of course, is figuring out the right location for a stone. If, as quite a few writers have suggested, the megaliths were put in the ground at carefully chosen points in much the same way that acupuncture needles are put in the human body, the way of mapping the meridians and the effects of the various points are the things that most need to be relearned!

comments.title: The Myth of the Land
comments.content:
Hello JMG!

I'd like to apologise for last week's out of boundaries message. Lessons learned not only of following instructions, but of being conscious of the action being done while you're doing it, both indispensable for magical practice.

That said, here goes my question again.

While meditatimg it ocurred to me to try a ritual to receive from the Three Kindreds, Gods, Ancestors and Spirits, in dreams and visions, a myth of the land where I live, as a way to know their view and will regarding the work that needs to be done here.

replies.title: Re: The Myth of the Land
replies.content: A lot of people are doing that kind of work these days, and it's appropriate. Most of the really good teachers of writing I know, when a student asked "Should I try to make it as a professional writer?" always answered "No." The reason was that you can't make it as a professional writer unless you have a passion for writing strong enough that you'll keep on writing even if somebody tells you not to.

replies.title: Re: House and Geomancy

You've got the house right, but the question's way too broad. Geomancy works best with questions that have a strong yes/no component; since there are only 16 figures, it can't tell you a lot about (say) what exactly to do to improve your tarot skills. replies.title: Re: Horoscopy
replies.content:
If you want to cast horoscopes by hand you're going to have to learn some mathematics; there ain't no way around that. Fortunately it's a lot less difficult than it seems at first glance, because most of the math has been systematized into tables, and you can simply look up the right numbers there.

Any of the pre-computer handbooks of astrology will tell you how to cast a chart. Two I've used extensively are Max Heindel's Simplified Scientific Astrology and Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson's Foundation of the Astrological Chart. Remember that you'll need to get printed ephemerides (tables of planetary placements) and tables of houses; I use the Rosicrucian ephemeris and a set of Placidus house tables, which you can get easily from the American Federation of Astrologers or from any astrological bookstore.

comments.title: curses
comments.content:
For the past several months I've dreamt of sinister, malevolent figures in my dreams around the eld of the moon. Sometimes this is accompanied by horrible trains of thought that feel like they're originating from outside of me. The regularity of this is what has caught my attention; it is always the few days before the new moon, and as the moon starts to wax this sense of organized malevolence noticeably disperses, while still remaining as background noise. I should note here that I do daily banishings.

A few days ago I sensed this and put on a protective amulet as per The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic and the thoughts that had been in my head stopped, very much like in your Weird of Hali books. I thought about Dion Fortune in Sane Occultism, how you can only defeat a mage of lower initiation. One character from my dreams is a real life mage who I have good reason to believe may be more of an initiate than I.

Somehow, I began to think about the Will as something that is outside the realms of normal consciousness, something outside of myself, and with these thoughts I felt myself immediately fill with the power of Will, discipline etc, and the sense of organized malevolence having power over me stopped as well. With this influx, I felt that I had power over the curse and I prayed that my defensive actions wouldn't amplify any blowback that the people who may be cursing me may experience. While I'm angered by the possibility that someone may be trying to harm me, I wished that their curse may be dispersed as much as possible similar as per what Dion Fortune suggests in Psychic Self Defense. Given the sorts of experiences I had been having, I could imagine that the blowback from this spell would be truly nasty, and the people doing it, according to my dreams, used to be my friends. Even if they were to have betrayed me heinously, they would still have a certain fond place in my heart, and I would still wish them the best. That being said, I wouldn't ever want to see them again.

Unfortunately, later that day I was "coincidentally" bitten by a deer tick. I've been taking large amounts of specific herbs, but for the past two days I've been rather under the weather. Of course this could all be coincidental, but my hackles are raised, given that these coincidences have been so ongoing and regular. That being said, I feel that the curse, or the intrusive malevolence has been dispelled like clouds lifting to reveal a shining sun, and that my experience of Will was genuine, and indeed, worked to dispel hostile forces, at least temporarily.

This experience has been going on now for something like a year, and I've been conscious of it's pattern for 3 or 4 months and it has been altogether utterly weird and dismaying. Of course, there is likely some Karmic Culmination going on, but nonetheless, given the lunar aspect and sense of recurrence I think that there may be more than that. I'm well aware that this I may be deluding myself. I don't consider myself an experienced Mage, or hardly a Mage at all really, and am aware that I can be over-imaginative, unbalanced in many ways and even foolish. That being said, I feel that in this experience I did indeed connect with something larger than myself, or perhaps a larger part of myself, since I can easily recall the feeling of connection to what I am calling Will, and that force is balanced and much stronger than my usual grade of will.

To close this comment:

1) I present this as information that at least some of the magic coming from the left seems to be perhaps more effective than the Trump binding spell, but to be coinciding with its schedule.

2) If I may ask a very general question, do you have any thoughts or suggestions concerning this JMG? This all seems so uncanny to my normal modes of consciousness that I would enormously appreciate your perspective on what I'm describing. I think taking stock of your perspective as an experienced Mage could help me very much to organize these experiences constructively. Quite honestly I feel shaken in many ways by this course of events and want to face the situation in the healthiest manner possible.

replies.title: Re: curses
replies.content:
Dion Fortune's comment about grades of initiation was made from within a very different understanding of initiation than the one we're used to using nowadays. She meant by it something considerably more spiritual in nature; the reason you can't defeat a mage of higher grade, in the sense she's using, is that a mage of higher grade is more in tune with the fundamental patterns of the cosmos, and the momentum of the entire universe is against you.

If somebody's throwing nasty magic at you -- and it does sound as though this is possible -- they're not that kind of initiate, and you can clean their clock with the right techniques.

I'd encourage you to use natural magic in addition to your daily banishings. You know the timing of the attack, so you can (for example) put a saucer of vinegar in the room where you sleep on the seven days before the new moon, don protective amulets at that time, and so on. Since a lot of the harassment comes in your dreams, consider making a protective amulet and putting it under your pillow or between the mattress and the box spring. There are plenty of good protective herbs out there, and you can put them to work readily enough to spare yourself some trouble.

replies.title: air and fire elements without flame
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

I am in no way qualified to advise you on the Grove Ritual, but my tradition also uses incense to represent elemental Air and sometimes does ritual in places where open flames and smoke are forbidden, such as in hotel rooms. In those situations, we use something else for Air. If it's a group ritual, we use a struck bell, chime or tingshas--the sound is a vibration of the air. For small groups or solitary practice, one might use a feather or a hand fan. These days, stores like Bed, Bath and Beyond sell aroma diffusers that don't involve heat; I don't use those because sometimes the scents are made of unnatural chemicals. As a more natural substitute, perhaps you could crush the leaves or needles of one of the aromatic trees in your forest to release its scent.

Fireless fire is a greater challenge if you don't want to use an electrical battery as a substitute. If it were me and no one was watching, I would use a a candle in a tall glass container (a novena candle, available in supermarkets), preferably set in an existing fire pit with the ground well cleared of combustible materials for some distance around it, and not leave it unattended. If a fire pit wasn't available, I would set the novena candle in a bowl or basin full of water before lighting it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The historic records of the Ogham, which were written in the Middle Ages, assign the sounds B,L,F,S,N to the first five Ogham fews. The traditional Irish name for the Ogham (and indeed for any alphabet) is Beith-Luis-Nion. A number of authors connected to the Druid Revival took that and noticed that if you put Nion (or Nuinn) in the third position, the resulting order of fews -- BLNFS, with "F" pronounced like V as it is in Irish -- gives you the name BeLeNVS, Belenus or Belenos, the ancient Celtic high god. Their theory is that this was the original order, and it was changed in Christian times to get rid of the name of a Pagan god. The version of the Ogham symbolism that derives from those Druid writers is the one I used in The Druidry Handbook; other people use the historic, Christianized version.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I’ve been struggling with whether to introduce this question, and how to phrase it, for a while. So, here goes.

Since childhood I’ve practiced—something—that I’ve been reluctant to call magic, because the details bear little resemblance to most of the specific practices you’ve described and discussed, here and in your other writings. (There are some interesting exceptions, that is to say isolated points of similarity, which might perhaps be a topic for future discussion.) Yet in overall intent and effect it seems to perfectly fit the definition of magic that you apply.

Until I learned that definition (initially, from one of your ADR posts that mentioned it), I thought of these practices primarily as childhood mental games that I refused to outgrow, and instead refined over time through trial and error, because they had effects that I continued to find engaging, comforting, or useful. I used my own invented terminology derived mostly from game design and fiction writing concepts. Most of the practices in question are variations of a common motif, which is altering the narratives that make up conscious experience by first understanding and sorting out their meanings, then finding ways to reshape those meanings. (Without attempting to alter objective facts, which doesn’t work for me.)

A simple example: “cold” can mean a form of discomfort; or it can mean a sensation of a fact about the ambient temperature. It’s easy to confuse the two, but I find that with practice, when I specify and clarify the latter “meaning,” the former, that is the discomfort, goes away. I do that by concentrating on awareness of the feeling of the cold air; by extending and diffusing myself (a visualization that would take a lot of words to try to describe in any meaningful way); and by refraining from thoughts (e.g. “when the damn bus finally comes I’ll be warm again”) and from unthinking actions (hunching over, turning away from the wind) that tend to connect the two meanings I’m trying to separate. All told, it works. (Up until the point significant physical effects i.e. hypothermia would begin. Wim Hof I’m not.)

The game-like qualities of these practices arise from the fact that narratives evolve; therefore they compete; therefore they resist. Mental focus and agility are needed to stay aware of and ahead of their adaptations. Exercises such as not thinking of a white bear (a children’s joke that, as a child, I took as a challenge), and practice with various forms of interactive storytelling, seem to help a lot.

In due course I can fill in a lot more detail and background on this, and I have many questions about it relative to occultism. But the question I should start with is… is discussion of it a welcome topic for this venue?

(I accept that it might not be, if for example it is not magic after all, or is magic of such a crude, trivial, impractical, or otherwise undesirable form as to not be worth discussing. I don’t want to play the role of crackpot approaching a physics professor to discuss his homemade mathematics-free alternative to Special Relativity. Nor of Monsieur Jourdain, Moliere’s titular bourgeois gentilhomme famous for being fatuously delighted to discover he’d been speaking prose all his life.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's what used to be known as mental magic or mentalism -- I tend to use the former term, because the latter's also been borrowed by one species of stage magician. It's a very effective approach for a whole range of things, and yes, it's certainly welcome here.

replies.content:
This is the sort of magic I grew up with, and that my mother's maternal line practiced as far back as my great-grandmother, and probably my great-great-grandmother. "Mental magic" is a fine term for it. It can indeed be made to work, as you have found out.

The old books I know of that come as close as anything to describing this form of magic are Charles Godfrey Leland's _The Mystic Will_ and William Walker Atkinson's _Mind Power: The Secret of Mental Magic_. It developed within the New Thought & Christian Science streams of alternate religious movements -- the same streams in which the girl who grew up to become Dion Fortune got her first tastes of magic as a child.

(And yes, this is the same Charles Godfrey Leland who gave _Aradia; or, The Gospel of the Witches_ to the world. Within the modern Witchcraft movement in the United States, one prominent figure was Leo Louis Martello. Martello issues cheap reprints of a number of older works that he thought Witches should read, among them not only Leland's _Aradia_, but Leland's _The Mystic Will_. So there is (or used to be) a strong thread of this sort of Mental Magic within the broad stream of Modern Witchcraft as well.

Several of us are gradually starting up an on-line discussion of these two books (and others by Atkinson, who also published as Yogi Ramacharaka and several other pseudonyms) on another dreamwidth forum:

williamwalkeratkinson.dreamwidth.org

comments.title: Magic in Hellenism
comments.content:
Hi Mr. Greer, hope you are well. I've run into a little disagreement with a pagan author, Angelo Nasios, who has made some claims I disagree with. For example, saying that "magic is irrelevant to Greeks today. Hellenism is not defined by magic", and in general recently has taken a rather dismissive and negative stance against magic. I don't really know where any of this is coming from, since he himself has authored books on Tarot and divination, but when I asked him about this, he said he was dismissive to "spells, charms, and enchantments. Trying to make Gods do things for you and the like. Old school magic."

My take on this is that he is taking a very narrow view of magic. I use the definition I learned from you and others here, "Magic is the art and science of causing change in consciousness in accordance with Will." As such, I consider prayer and ritual to be forms of magic as well.

But I think it is very dismissive to say that magic is simply a way to 'get' something from the Gods. I started out, as so many do, in a neopagan, Wiccan circle, and while I am now a general polytheist, I think this attitude pervades many other neopagans. Do a spell or charm and get something in return, sort of like a Cosmic Vending Machine. I think this is what he was getting at. It's a bad habit to do something like that, not expecting to put in any real effort or work, and expect results, in addition to your Raspberry Jam Principle. But I also think it's throwing the baby out with the bathwater, to disregard Ancient Greek magical rites as something just a relic from an archaic past, and not somehow connected to modern Hellenic polytheism. Does that make sense?

replies.title: Re: Magic in Hellenism
replies.content: With every religion I've ever heard of, you get people who insist that magic has no place in it. There are even people making that claim about Wicca these days, for heaven's sake! If Mr. Nasios doesn't want to deal with magic, he has every right to avoid the subject, but don't let his hangups interfere with your choices.

replies.title: Re: Magic in Hellenism
replies.content:
Certainly the Ancient Greeks of did lots and lots of magic, and some of their later handbooks and grimoires have even come down to our days, and have been translated into English by various scholars.

It strikes me as a "hangover" from rejected Christianity when some Pagan author claims that magic has no part in this or that Pagan religion. As far as Ancient Paganism goes, it is a claim that won;t stand up to scholarly examination.

comments.title: Dion Fortune
comments.content: You've recommended several of Dion Fortune's books, and I've been keeping my eyes open for them at the local used bookstores. Out of curiosity, are there any titles of hers you would NOT recommend, or at least would caution beginner-to-intermediate practitioners against?

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune
replies.content:
I recommend reading everything she wrote. I don't recommend practicing everything she suggests! The famous exercise for redirecting sexual energy in The Problem of Purity simply doesn't work for most people, and a fair amount of her sexual advice there and in The Esoteric Philosophy of Love and Marriage, has more to do with her own personal hangups and those of her society than anything else; her later reflections in The Circuit of Force are more realistic. I also don't recommend taking her writings on occult history too seriously -- as a historian, she was a really good mage.

Fortune wrote at a time when it was standard practice in magical circles never, but never, to give out practical instruction. That's changed, and a good thing, too -- and one consequence is that her practical advice is by and large very weak tea. Thus I encourage reading her books for the philosophy and theory, which are by and large well worth attention, and getting your practical lessons from some more modern and more forthright source -- for example, my books. ;-)

Hi JMG,
Last week, I read an article on possible evidence for a new ninth planet. It's all very tentative, of course, but it made me think to what you've written about Pluto here.

So, it seems like if a new ninth planet is discovered, in astrological terms we'd start seeing a new influence here on Earth.
Do you have any notion of how astrologers would go about sorting that influence out from the multitude of other factors already in play? I have to imagine the task would take several decades.

Also, if planets have ruling gods, intelligences, and spirits, what does that mean in relation to Pluto, or a new planet? Did Pluto's governing entities stop by for a 'quick' check-in, and now they're off again, before the new guy shows up?

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: Re: New 9th planet?
replies.content:
Well, look at the way that astrologers figured out the influences of Uranus, Neptune, and Pluto. They started noting the positions of those planets in the whole range of astrological charts, and paid attention to the effects of aspects and planetary positions. Over time, that allowed them to get a sense of what influences the planets had, and to make predictions on that basis. It's not fast -- a couple of decades of hard work at minimum -- but it seems to work fairly well.

As for the governing energies and entities of Pluto and Ceres, and other temporary planets, my guess is that they briefly concerned themselves with matters on Earth, and then turned their attention elsewhere. The solar system's a big place, and humanity's not that important.

replies.title: Re: Decompressing From Magic Events
replies.content: Hmm! As an introvert with Aspergers syndrome, I have the opposite reaction to events like that -- I typically end up exhausted and depressed, and getting back to my own private space is an immense relief. Does anyone have any suggestions to offer?

replies.title: Re: Decompressing From Magic Events
replies.content: You can lie flat on the ground, either prone or supine, oriented to the Directions (Head: North; feet: South) wih arms spread wide. Hands either holding a pair of stones (common quartz is good) or gently grasping the grass of the field. Allow excess energy to drain from your body into the Earth to nourish and revitalize it. If no outdoors site available, or cannot get down so low due to disability, sit in a chair and imagine draining excess energy down from head into feet and continuing till the flow reaches the surface of the earth. Breathe in calm, breathe out jittery excitement. Five breaths ought to do it, one for each Direction and one for the center Spirit. If you commonly address the Six Directions (Above and Below), include them also. Use precautions for banishing and cleansing the sacred space, as usual.
